column1: # Terms are pinned to the atom's three LO parts:
#   3. Apply safe-practice rules for documentation and access
# The EHR-vs-EMR distinction and interoperability framing anchor LO part 1.

column4: and order anchor LO part 2 as

column3: and patient portal anchor LO part 3

column2: and ICD/CPT coding live in other atoms.
A digital, longitudinal record of a patient's health information designed to be shared across providers and care settings.
column4: An electronic health record follows the patient from the primary-care clinic to the emergency department to the specialist.

column2: A digital chart that lives inside one facility's system; the older, single-site precursor to the shareable EHR.
column4: An electronic medical record captures what happened at one clinic, but does not travel with the patient to another provider.

column2: The section of the chart that holds a patient's active and resolved diagnoses in one running list.
column4: A clinician opens the problem list first to see what conditions the patient is being treated for.

column2: A dated, signed narrative entry that records what the clinician observed, thought, and did during one encounter.
column4: Every visit produces at least one progress note that becomes part of the permanent record.

column2: A grid in the chart that records repeated measurements — vitals, intake/output, pain scores — across time.
column4: Nurses record vital signs on a flowsheet so the whole team can see trends at a glance.

column2: A clinician's written instruction in the chart — for a medication, lab, imaging study, diet, or activity level — that other staff carry out.
column4: An order for a chest X-ray routes automatically to radiology once the clinician signs it.

column2: An automatic log that records which user opened, viewed, or changed each chart and when.
column4: Because of the audit trail, every click in a patient's chart can be reviewed later.

column2: An access-control rule that grants each user only the chart permissions their job requires.
column4: Role-based access means a registration clerk can update demographics but cannot view a progress note.

column2: A documentation mistake created when text carried forward from an older note no longer describes the current patient or visit.
column4: A copy-paste error can leave last week's chest pain description inside today's note for a patient who has a new complaint.

column2: A documentation or order mistake caused by entering information into the chart of a patient who is not the one in front of the clinician.
column4: A wrong-patient error often starts when two charts are open at once and the clinician types into the wrong window.

column2: The decreased attention clinicians pay to pop-up warnings after seeing too many non-critical alerts in the EHR.
column4: Alert fatigue sets in when the same low-value warning fires on every patient, so a real drug-interaction alert gets clicked past.

column2: A secure website or app that lets a patient read parts of their own EHR, message the care team, and view results.
column4: Through the patient portal, a patient can read the same lab result the clinician is reading.

column2: The ability of different EHR systems to exchange and use each other's patient data.
column4: Interoperability is why a specialist across town can pull up the imaging already done at the primary-care clinic.
